w:r.w:t: Based on "How Much Land Does a Man Need?" by Leo Tolstoy
Premium Study Resource for Students • Aligned with Curriculum Objectives
How Much Land Does a Man Need?
The dangers of greed and the illusion of worldly possessions
2. What might be the main conflict?
3. What moral lessons come from stories about desire for "more"?
This story develops critical thinking by:
Encouraging readers to analyze character motivations and consequences
Prompting reflection on universal human desires vs. true needs
Using irony and symbolism to convey deeper philosophical truths
He felt his land was "not much different than any other landowners'"—he wanted more.
A passing traveler told him about
, where "rye sown on it grew as high as a horse."
: Pahom sold his land, homestead, and cattle at a profit and moved his family to the new settlement.
Part 2: The Cycle of Desire Continues
At first, Pahom was pleased with his new, larger holdings.
He grew tired of renting and wanted to
A land dealer told him about the
: "thirteen thousand acres of land, all for only one thousand rubles."
: "All one need do is to make friends with the chiefs."
Part 3: The Bashkir Proposal – A Dangerous Bargain
Pahom traveled with gifts (dressing gowns, carpets, tea) to meet the Bashkirs.
The Bashkir chief offered an unusual deal:
"Choose whatever piece of land you like... Our price is always the same: one thousand rubles a day."
"As much as you can go round on your feet in a day is yours... If you don't return on the same day to the spot whence you started, your money is lost."
by digging holes and piling turf; the Bashkirs would later plough from hole to hole.
Part 4: The Walk – Greed Takes Over
Pahom started at sunrise, walking toward the east.
As he walked, he kept thinking:
"The further one goes, the better the land seems."
He extended his route to include a
good for flax, making his path longer.
By noon, he was tired but pushed on, thinking:
"An hour to suffer, a lifetime to live."
As the sun began to descend, Pahom realized he had
Exhausted, feet cut and bruised, legs failing, he
threw away his coat, boots, flask, and cap
The Bashkirs shouted encouragement; the chief laughed, holding his sides.
Part 6: The Tragic Irony of the Ending
With his last strength, Pahom rushed up the hill as the sun touched the horizon.
He reached the top, saw the fox-fur cap marking the start, and
His servant buried him in a grave
six feet from head to heels
"Six feet from his head to his heels was all he needed."
Pahom repeatedly seeks more land despite having enough: "He wanted to sow more wheat, but had not enough land of his own." His greed ultimately costs him his life.
Pahom dies just after acquiring the chance to own vast land; the story ends: "Six feet from his head to his heels was all he needed."
Pahom is never satisfied: first with 40 acres, then with his new settlement, then with rented land. His inability to be content drives the tragedy.
The sun's setting symbolizes life's limits; Pahom's physical exhaustion shows the body's boundaries against unlimited ambition.
Pahom believes more land = more prosperity, but the story reveals that true need is minimal: a grave of six feet.
"Six feet from his head to his heels was all he needed."
Highlights the futility of Pahom's greed; powerful moral conclusion
The sun = time/life limit; the spade = tool of labor and burial
Adds layers of meaning; connects plot to universal truths
Chief's warning: "If you don't return... your money is lost."
Builds tension; hints at tragic outcome
"His breast was working like a blacksmith's bellows, his heart was beating like a hammer"
Vividly conveys physical strain and desperation
Makes abstract vice tangible and memorable
Pahom's repeated thoughts: "The further one goes, the better the land seems"
Emphasizes escalating desire and loss of reason
Q1: What moral/lesson do you learn from the story of Pahom?
greed and endless desire for more can destroy a person
. Pahom had enough land to live comfortably but was never satisfied. His pursuit of "just a little more"
led to exhaustion and death. The final line—"Six feet from his head to his heels was all he needed"—reminds us that true human needs are modest, and worldly possessions cannot be taken beyond death.
Q2: What urged Pahom to abandon his well-settled life and desire for more land?

. He heard from a traveler that land beyond the Volga was incredibly fertile ("rye sown on it grew as high as a horse"). He also learned from a land dealer that Bashkir land was available at an unbelievably low price. These reports, combined with his belief that "more land = more prosperity," motivated him to seek greater wealth.
Q3: Describe what thoughts Pahom had about grasping the land.
"The further one goes, the better the land seems."
"It would be a pity to leave that [damp hollow] out... Flax would grow well there."
"I have made the sides too long. I must make this one shorter."
"Though it will make my land lopsided, I must hurry back in a straight line now."
These show he prioritized claiming land over safety, reason, and physical limits.
Q4: What strategy did Pahom use to mark the land that he covered while walking on it?
As instructed by the Bashkir chief, Pahom carried a spade and
dug a hole at every turning, piling up pieces of turf to make the mark more visible
. Later, the Bashkirs would plough from hole to hole to define the boundary of the land he claimed.
Q5: How did Pahom's attitude change, from being confident to fear of losing both life and land, as he went on in measuring the land?
: He started calmly, thinking "I must lose no time," and walked "neither slowly nor quickly."
: As the day warmed and he extended his route, he began to hurry, thinking about turning back.
: When he realized the sun was setting and he was far from the goal,
"He was seized with terror lest he should die of the strain."
He ran, threw away belongings, and thought:
"I have grasped too much! What a fool I have been!"
Q6: Do you think Pahom was pushing himself too hard while walking to cover as much land as possible? How?
Yes. The text shows Pahom ignored clear warning signs:
He was "done up with the heat," with "bare feet... cut and bruised" and "legs began to fail."
He longed to rest but refused:
"it was impossible if he meant to get back before sunset."
"His breast was working like a blacksmith's bellows... his legs were giving way as if they did not belong to him."
His refusal to accept limits led directly to his death.
Q7: Why was the Chief of Bashkirs laughing on Pahom's efforts?
The chief laughed because he recognized the

of Pahom's greed. While Pahom struggled desperately to claim vast land, the chief knew that no matter how much Pahom gained, he could only use a tiny portion in life—and in death, he needed only six feet. The chief's laughter underscores the story's moral: worldly ambition is ultimately meaningless.
Q8: What does the ending of the story reveal about Pahom's character?
greed blinded him to reason and self-preservation
. Even as he faced death, his focus remained on securing land:
"There is plenty of land," thought he, "but will God let me live on it?"
His tragic end shows that desire, unchecked by wisdom or contentment, leads to self-destruction.
1. Pahom was a wealthy landowner who was satisfied with his land.
2. Pahom heard about fertile land beyond the Volga from a passing traveler.
"Pahom heard from a man passing by his house about the land beyond the Volga."
3. After selling his land and cattle, Pahom moved his family to the new settlement.
"Pahom sold his land, homestead and cattle... and moved his family to the new settlement."
4. Pahom was content with his new land and did not want any more.
"When he got used to it, he began to think that even here he was not satisfied."
5. A land dealer told Pahom that he could buy land from the Bashkirs for a very low price.
"He had bought thirteen thousand acres of land, all for only one thousand rubbles."
6. The Bashkirs sold land by the acre, and Pahom knew exactly how much he could buy.
"We sell it by the day, as much as you can go round on your feet in a day is yours."
7. Pahom was required to return to the starting point before sunset to keep the land he covered.
"Before the sun sets you must return to the place you started from. All the land you cover will be yours."
8. Pahom took many breaks during his land measuring journey.
He sat briefly to eat/drink but refused to lie down: "he did not lie down thinking that if he did, he might fall asleep."
9. Pahom was able to successfully return to the starting point before the sun set.
He reached the spot just as/after the sun set; he died immediately upon arrival.
10. Pahom died of exhaustion just as he reached the cap marking the end of his journey.
"He fell forward and reached the fur cap with his hands outstretched... Pahom was dead!"
1. One of the following objects used as metaphor in this story is:
2. Pahom takes ______ with him as he starts measuring the land.
3. The story expresses an irony on human's:
4. Which of the following proverbs best fits the message of the story?
B) What you give, repays you.
C) Pride goes before a fall.
D) Greed is a bottomless pit.
5. Which of the following characteristics are found in Pahom?
6. How does Pahom mark the land as he walks?
A) He digs holes and piles up turf
D) He digs holes and sows seeds
7. "Though it will make my land lopsided, I must hurry back in a straight line now...." The underlined word means:
8. The length of Pahom's grave at the end of the story symbolizes:
A) His will for a simple burial
C) The irony of his greed for land
D) The Bashkirs' respect for his achievement
9. Why did Pahom decide to seek out the Bashkirs' land?
A) He wanted to escape his family
B) He heard it was available at an incredibly low price
C) He was forced to move by the government
D) He wanted to become a chief
10. What happened to Pahom just as he reached the starting point?
C) The Bashkirs refused to give him the land
D) He decided to walk further
11. What does "grew tired of" mean in context?
B) Became bored or dissatisfied with
12. In context, what does "to reckon it out" mean?
A) To calculate or figure out
13. What does "go round on your feet" mean?
B) To walk around a boundary
D) To search for something lost
14. What does "done up with" mean in "He was done up with the heat"?
15. What does "giving way" mean describing Pahom's legs?
16. Why does Tolstoy include the detail that Pahom threw away his coat, boots, and flask while running?
A) To show he was careless with belongings
B) To emphasize his desperation and single-minded focus on reaching the goal
C) To suggest the Bashkirs would steal his items
D) To indicate he no longer needed protection from weather
17. How does the story's ending strengthen its moral message?
A) By showing that hard work always pays off
B) By using stark irony: Pahom gains the chance to own vast land but needs only six feet in death
C) By proving that the Bashkirs were tricksters
D) By suggesting that Pahom should have rested more
18. What lesson about desire can modern readers apply from Pahom's story?
A) Always pursue more to achieve success
B) Recognize when "enough" is enough; unchecked desire can lead to loss of what truly matters
C) Avoid all risks to stay safe
D) Trust strangers who offer good deals
19. Which quote BEST shows Pahom's growing greed during his walk?
B) "The further one goes, the better the land seems."
C) "An hour to suffer, a lifetime to live."
D) "What shall I do... I have grasped too much!"
20. Why is the Bashkir chief's laughter significant at the story's end?
A) He is mocking Pahom's failure
B) He recognizes the universal truth that human ambition is ultimately futile against mortality
C) He is happy to keep Pahom's money
D) He is relieved Pahom won't claim more land
"He grew tired of having to rent other people's land"
"Pahom left his family to look after the homestead"
"We do not know how to reckon it out"
"As much as you can go round on your feet in a day is yours"
To travel around; complete a circuit
"In a day, you can get round a large tract of land"
Scattered or went separate ways for
"He saw through the open door that the dawn was breaking"
"The sun's rays had hardly flashed above the horizon"
"He was done up with the heat"
"His legs were giving way as if they did not belong to him"
"As much as you can go round on your feet in a day is yours... If you don't return... your money is lost."
Central bargain; symbolizes life's limits and consequences of overreaching
"The further one goes, the better the land seems."
Shows how greed distorts judgment and fuels endless desire
"An hour to suffer, a lifetime to live."
Rationalization that leads to self-destruction; warns against sacrificing present well-being for uncertain future gain
"I have grasped too much! What a fool I have been!"
Moment of tragic realization; too late to change course
"Six feet from his head to his heels was all he needed."
Powerful ironic conclusion; reveals true human need vs. worldly ambition

Pahom dissatisfied with 40 acres → Hears of fertile land → Moves to new settlement → Still wants more → Learns of Bashkir deal → Accepts dangerous terms → Greed extends his route → Sun sets, he rushes back → Reaches start but dies → Buried in 6-foot grave: "all he needed"
Always support answers with specific phrases from the text.
For theme questions, focus on: greed vs. contentment, irony of possessions, human limits.
When analyzing symbols, connect: land = desire, sun = time/life, grave = mortality.
Use quotes to strengthen critical thinking responses.
Remember: The story is a fable—look for the universal moral lesson.
